Sets Round Trip Time on socket if available\n2. Uses connection balancer to pick target worker handler\n3. If load balanced to different handler, calls `target_handler.post()`\n4. NETWORK FILTER INSTANTIATION\n\n**ListenerImpl::createNetworkFilterChain** (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/listener_impl.cc:942-952`):\n```cpp\nbool ListenerImpl::createNetworkFilterChain(\n    Network::Connection& connection, \n    const Filter::NetworkFilterFactoriesList& filter_factories) {\n  if (!Configuration::FilterChainUtility::buildFilterChain(connection, filter_factories)) {\n    return false;\n  }\n  return true;\n}\n```\n\n**FilterChainUtility::buildFilterChain** (`/workspace/source/server/configuration_impl.h:75-91`):\n- Takes network filter factories from `FilterChain::networkFilterFactories()`\n- Creates each network filter instance via factory callback\n- Adds filters to the connection's filter manager\n- Returns false if any filter creation fails\n\n**FilterChain Implementation** (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/filter_chain_manager_impl.h:86-122`):\n- `networkFilterFactories()` - returns `Filter::NetworkFilterFactoriesList&` (line 103-105)\n- Contains vector of `FilterConfigProviderPtr<FilterFactoryCb>`\n\n### 6. ACTIVE TCP CONNECTION CREATION\n\n**newActiveConnection** (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/active_tcp_listener.cc:149-164`):\n- Called after network filters are instantiated\n- Creates `ActiveTcpConnection` wrapper\n- Gets or creates `ActiveConnections` container for the filter chain\n- Adds connection callbacks for lifecycle management\n- Moves connection to connections list\n\n### KEY CLASS HIERARCHY\n\n```\nTcpListenerCallbacks (interface)\n  └── ActiveTcpListener\n       └── OwnedActiveStreamListenerBase\n            └── ActiveStreamListenerBase\n                 └── Put here for readability.\n   344→    struct State {\n   345→      State()\n   346→          : codec_saw_local_complete_(false), codec_encode_complete_(false),\n   347→            on_reset_stream_called_(false), is_zombie_stream_(false), successful_upgrade_(false),\n   348→            is_internally_destroyed_(false), is_internally_created_(false), is_tunneling_(false),\n   349→            decorated_propagate_(true), deferred_to_next_io_iteration_(false),\n   350→            deferred_end_stream_(false) {}\n   351→\n   352→      // It's possibly for the codec to see the completed response but not fully\n   353→      // encode it.\n   354→      bool codec_saw_local_complete_ : 1; // This indicates that local is complete as the completed\n   355→                                          // response has made its way to the codec.\n   356→      bool codec_encode_complete_ : 1;    // This indicates that the codec has\n   357→                                          // completed encoding the response.\n   358→      bool on_reset_stream_called_ : 1;   // Whether the stream has been reset.\n   359→      bool is_zombie_stream_ : 1;         // Whether stream is waiting for signal\n   360→                                          // the underlying codec to be destroyed.\n   361→      bool successful_upgrade_ : 1;\n   362→\n   363→      // True if this stream was the original externally created stream, but was\n   364→      // destroyed as part of internal redirect.\n   365→      bool is_internally_destroyed_ : 1;\n   366→      // True if this stream is internally created. Currently only used for\n   367→      // internal redirects or other streams created via recreateStream().\n   368→      bool is_internally_created_ : 1;\n   369→\n   370→      // True if the response headers indicate a successful upgrade or connect\n   371→      // response.\n   372→      bool is_tunneling_ : 1;\n   373→\n   374→      bool decorated_propagate_ : 1;\n   375→\n   376→      // Indicates that sending headers to the filter manager is deferred to the\n   377→      // next I/O cycle. If data or trailers are received when this flag is set\n   378→      // they are deferred too.\n   379→      // TODO(yanavlasov): encapsulate the entire state of deferred streams into a separate\n   380→      // structure, so it can be atomically created and cleared.\n   381→      bool deferred_to_next_io_iteration_ : 1;\n   382→      bool deferred_end_stream_ : 1;\n   383→    };\n   384→\n   385→    bool canDestroyStream() const {\n   386→      return state_.on_reset_stream_called_ || state_.codec_encode_complete_ ||\n   387→             state_.is_internally_destroyed_;\n   388→    }\n   389→\n   390→    // Per-stream idle timeout callback.\n   391→    void onIdleTimeout();\n   392→    // Per-stream request timeout callback.\n   393→    void onRequestTimeout();\n   394→    // override;\n   404→\n   405→    // Return local port of the connection.\n   406→    uint32_t localPort();\n   407→\n   408→    friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, const ActiveStream& s) {\n   409→      s.dumpState(os);\n   410→      return os;\n   411→    }\n   412→\n   413→    Tracing::CustomTagMap& getOrMakeTracingCustomTagMap() {\n   414→      if (tracing_custom_tags_ == nullptr) {\n   415→        tracing_custom_tags_ = std::make_unique<Tracing::CustomTagMap>();\n   416→      }\n   417→      return *tracing_custom_tags_;\n   418→    }\n   419→\n   420→    // Note: this method is a noop unless ENVOY_ENABLE_UHV is defined\n   421→    // Call header validator extension to validate request header map after it was deserialized.\n   422→    // If header map failed validation, it sends an error response and returns false.\n   423→    bool validateHeaders();\n   424→\n   425→    // Note: this method is a noop unless ENVOY_ENABLE_UHV is defined\n   426→    // Call header validator extension to validate the request trailer map after it was\n   427→    // deserialized. If the trailer map failed validation, this method does the following:\n   428→    // 1. For H/1 it sends 400 response and returns false.\n   429→    // 2. For H/2 and H/3 it resets the stream (without error response). Issue #24735 is filed to\n   430→    //    harmonize this behavior with H/1.\n   431→    // 3. If the `stream_error_on_invalid_http_message` is set to `false` (it is by default) in the\n   432→    // HTTP connection manager configuration, then the entire connection is closed.\n   433→    bool validateTrailers(RequestTrailerMap& trailers);\n   434→\n   435→    std::weak_ptr<bool> stillAlive() { return {still_alive_}; }\n   436→\n   437→    // Dispatch deferred headers, body and trailers to the filter manager.\n   438→    // Return true if this stream was deferred and dispatched pending headers, body and trailers (if\n   439→    // present). Return false if this stream was not deferred.\n   440→    bool onDeferredRequestProcessing();\n   441→\n   442→    ConnectionManagerImpl& connection_manager_;\n   443→    OptRef<const TracingConnectionManagerConfig> connection_manager_tracing_config_;\n   444→    // TODO(snowp): It might make sense to move this to the FilterManager to avoid storing it in\n   445→    // both locations, then refer to the FM when doing stream logs.\n   446→    const uint64_t stream_id_;\n   447→\n   448→    RequestHeaderMapSharedPtr request_headers_;\n   449→    RequestTrailerMapPtr request_trailers_;\n   450→\n   451→    ResponseHeaderMapPtr informational_headers_;\n   452→    ResponseHeaderMapSharedPtr response_headers_;\n   453→    ResponseTrailerMapSharedPtr response_trailers_;\n   454→\n   455→    // Note: The FM must outlive the above headers, as they are possibly accessed during filter\n   456→    // destruction.\n   457→    DownstreamFilterManager filter_manager_;\n   458→\n   459→    Tracing::SpanPtr active_span_;\n   460→    ResponseEncoder* response_encoder_{};\n   461→    Stats::TimespanPtr request_response_timespan_;\n   462→    // Per-stream idle timeout. This timer gets reset whenever activity occurs on the stream, and,\n   463→    // when triggered, will close the stream.\n   464→    Event::TimerPtr stream_idle_timer_;\n   465→    // Per-stream request timeout. This timer is enabled when the stream is created and disabled\n   466→    // when the stream ends. If triggered, it will close the stream.\n   467→    Event::TimerPtr request_timer_;\n   468→    // Per-stream request header timeout. This timer is enabled when the stream is created and\n   469→    // disabled when the downstream finishes sending headers. If triggered, it will close the\n   470→    // stream.\n   471→    Event::TimerPtr request_header_timer_;\n   472→    // Per-stream alive duration. This timer is enabled once when the stream is created and, if\n   473→    // triggered, will close the stream.\n   474→    Event::TimerPtr max_stream_duration_timer_;\n   475→    // Per-stream access log flush duration. This timer is enabled once when the stream is created\n   476→    // and will log to all access logs once per trigger.\n   477→    Event::TimerPtr access_log_flush_timer_;\n   478→\n   479→    std::chrono::milliseconds idle_timeout_ms_{};\n   480→    State state_;\n   481→\n   482→    // Snapshot of the route configuration at the time of request is started. This is used to ensure\n   483→    // that the same route configuration is used throughout the lifetime of the request. This\n   484→    // snapshot will be cleared when the cached route is blocked. Because after that we will not\n   485→    // refresh the cached route and release this snapshot can help to release the memory when the\n   486→    // route configuration is updated frequently and the request is long-lived.\n   487→    Router::ConfigConstSharedPtr snapped_route_config_;\n   488→    Router::ScopedConfigConstSharedPtr snapped_scoped_routes_config_;\n   489→    // This is used to track the route that has been cached in the request. And we will keep this\n   490→    // route alive until the request is finished.\n   491→    absl::optional<Router::RouteConstSharedPtr> cached_route_;\n   492→    // This is used to track whether the route has been blocked. If the route is blocked, we can not\n   493→    // clear it or refresh it.\n   494→    bool route_cache_blocked_{false};\n   495→    // This is used to track routes that have been cleared from the request. By this way, all the\n   496→    // configurations that have been used in the processing of the request will be alive until the\n   497→    // request is finished.\n   498→    // For example, if a filter stored a per-route config in the decoding phase and may try to\n   499→    // use it in the encoding phase, but the route is cleared and refreshed by another decoder\n   500→    // filter, we must keep the per-route config alive to avoid use-after-free.\n   501→    // Note that we assume that the number of routes that have been cleared is small. So we use\n   502→    // inline vector to avoid heap allocation. If this assumption is wrong, we should consider using\n   503→    // a list or other data structures.\n   504→    //\n   505→    // TODO(wbpcode): This is a helpless compromise. The `sendPendingFrames()` is no-op and\n   650→   *    always returns success to avoid reentering nghttp2 library.\n   651→   * 2. Server codec only. dispatching_ == false.\n   652→   *    The `sendPendingFrames()` returns the status of the protocol constraint checks. Outbound\n   653→   *    frame accounting is performed.\n   654→   * 3. dispatching_ == false. The `sendPendingFrames()` always returns success. No outbound\n   655→   *    frame accounting.\n   656→   *\n   657→   * TODO(yanavlasov): harmonize behavior for cases 2, 3.\n   658→   */\n   659→  Status sendPendingFrames();\n   660→\n   661→ 703→  bool use_oghttp2_library_;\n   704→\n   705→  // If deferred processing, the streams will be in LRU order based on when the\n   706→  // stream encoded to the http2 connection. The\n   729→  // `codec_callback_status_` is valid iff nghttp call returned NGHTTP2_ERR_CALLBACK_FAILURE.\n   730→  Status codec_callback_status_;\n   731→\n   732→  // Set if the type of frame that is about to be sent is PING or SETTINGS with the ACK flag set, or\n   733→  // RST_STREAM.\n   734→  bool is_outbound_flood_monitored_control_frame_ = 0;\n   735→  ProtocolConstraints protocol_constraints_;\n   736→\n   737→  // For the flood mitigation to work the onSend callback must be called once for each outbound\n   738→  // frame. The\n   739→  // Http2FloodMitigationTest.* tests in test/integration/http2_integration_test.cc will break if\n   740→  // this changes in the future. Also it is important that onSend does not do partial writes, as the\n   741→  // nghttp2 library will keep calling this callback to write the rest of the frame.\n   742→  ssize_t onSend(const uint8_t* We omit this check if we're\n    36→  // the first filter, since the above check handles that case.\n    37→  //\n    38→  // We compare against the previous filter to avoid multiple filter iterations The connection manager bases further\n    36→ * filter invocations on the return code of the previous filter.\n    37→ */\n    38→enum class FilterHeadersStatus {\n    39→  // Continue filter chain iteration.\n    40→  Continue,\n    41→  // Do not iterate for headers on any of the remaining filters in the chain.\n    42→  //\n    43→  // Returning FilterDataStatus::Continue from decodeData()/encodeData() or calling\n    44→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() MUST be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n    45→  //\n    46→  // Note that if a local reply was sent, no further iteration for headers as well as data and\n    47→  // trailers for the current filter and the filters following will happen. A local reply can be\n    48→  // triggered via sendLocalReply() or encodeHeaders().\n    49→  StopIteration,\n    50→  // Continue headers iteration to remaining filters, but delay ending the stream. This status MUST\n    51→  // NOT be returned when end_stream is already set to false.\n    52→  //\n    53→  // Used when a filter wants to add a body to a headers-only request/response, but this body is not\n    54→  // readily available. Delaying end_stream allows the filter to add the body once it's available\n    55→  // without stopping headers iteration.\n    56→  //\n    57→  // The filter is responsible to continue the stream by providing a body through calling\n    58→  // injectDecodedDataToFilterChain()/injectEncodedDataToFilterChain(), possibly multiple times\n    59→  // if the body needs to be divided into several chunks. The filter may need to handle\n    60→  // watermark events when injecting a body, see:\n    61→  // https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/docs/flow_control.md.\n    62→  //\n    63→  // The last call to inject data MUST have end_stream set to true to conclude the stream.\n    64→  // If the filter cannot provide a body the stream should be reset.\n    65→  //\n    66→  // Adding a body through calling addDecodedData()/addEncodedData() then\n    67→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() is currently NOT supported and causes an assert failure.\n    68→  //\n    69→  // Adding trailers in this scenario is currently NOT supported.\n    70→  //\n    71→  // The filter MUST NOT attempt to continue the stream without providing a body using\n    72→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding().\n    73→  //\n    74→  // TODO(yosrym93): Support adding a body in this case by calling addDecodedData()/addEncodedData()\n    75→  // then continueDecoding()/continueEncoding(). To support this a new FilterManager::IterationState\n    76→  // needs to be added and set when a filter returns this status in\n    77→  // FilterManager::decodeHeaders/FilterManager::encodeHeaders()\n    78→  // Currently, when a filter returns this, the IterationState is Continue. This causes ASSERTs in\n    79→  // FilterManager::commonContinue() to fail when continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() is called;\n    80→  // due to trying to continue iteration when the IterationState is already Continue.\n    81→  // In this case, a different ASSERT will be needed to make sure the filter does not try to\n    82→  // continue without adding a body first.\n    83→  //\n    84→  // TODO(yosrym93): Support adding trailers in this case by implementing new functions to inject\n    85→  // trailers, similar to the inject data functions.\n    86→  ContinueAndDontEndStream,\n    87→  // Do not iterate for headers as well as data and trailers for the current filter and the filters\n    88→  // following, and buffer body data for later dispatching. Said data is immediately passed off to the\n    45→ * UpstreamFilterManager, which passes each item through the filter chain until\n    46→ * it arrives at the last filter in the chain, the UpstreamCodecFilter. If the upstream\n    47→ * stream is not established, the UpstreamCodecFilter returns StopAllIteration, and the\n    48→ * FilterManager will buffer data, using watermarks to push back to the router\n    49→ * filter if buffers become overrun. When an upstream connection is established,\n    50→ * the UpstreamCodecFilter will send data upstream.\n    51→ *\n    52→ * On the new response path, payload arrives from upstream via the UpstreamCodecFilter's\n    53→ * CodecBridge. It is passed off directly to the FilterManager, traverses the\n    54→ * filter chain, and completion is signaled via the\n    55→ * UpstreamRequestFilterManagerCallbacks's encode[X] functions. These somewhat\n    56→ * confusingly pass through the UpstreamRequest's legacy decode[X] functions\n    57→ * (required due to the UpstreamToDownstream interface, but will be renamed once\n    58→ * the classic mode is deprecated), and are finally passed to the router via the\n    59→ * RouterFilterInterface onUpstream[X] functions.\n    60→ *\n    61→ * There is some required communication between the UpstreamRequest and\n    62→ * UpstreamCodecFilter. significant figures on the histogram would be nice.\n   132→uint64_t FilterUtility::percentageOfTimeout(const std::chrono::milliseconds response_time,\n   133→                                            const std::chrono::milliseconds timeout) {\n   134→  // Timeouts of 0 are considered infinite. If there is we take that.\n   189→  // Otherwise if the request is gRPC and a maximum gRPC timeout is configured we use the timeout\n   190→  // in the gRPC headers (or infinity when gRPC headers have no timeout), but cap that timeout to\n   191→  // the configured maximum gRPC timeout (which may also be infinity, represented by a 0 value),\n   192→  // or the default from the route config otherwise.\n   193→  TimeoutData timeout;\n   194→  if (!route.usingNewTimeouts()) {\n   195→    if (grpc_request && route.maxGrpcTimeout()) {\n   196→      const std::chrono::milliseconds max_grpc_timeout = route.maxGrpcTimeout().value();\n   197→      auto header_timeout = Grpc::Common::getGrpcTimeout(request_headers);\n   198→      std::chrono::milliseconds grpc_timeout =\n   199→          header_timeout ? header_timeout.value() : std::chrono::milliseconds(0);\n   200→      if (route.grpcTimeoutOffset()) {\n   201→        // We only apply the offset if it won't result in grpc_timeout hitting 0 or below, as\n   202→        // setting it to 0 means infinity and a negative timeout makes no sense.\n   203→        const auto offset = *route.grpcTimeoutOffset();\n   204→        if (offset < grpc_timeout) {\n   205→          grpc_timeout -= offset;\n   206→        }\n   207→      }\n   208→\n   209→      // Cap gRPC timeout to the configured maximum considering that 0 means infinity.\n   210→      if (max_grpc_timeout != std::chrono::milliseconds(0) &&\n   211→          (grpc_timeout == std::chrono::milliseconds(0) || grpc_timeout > max_grpc_timeout)) {\n   212→        grpc_timeout = max_grpc_timeout;\n   213→      }\n   214→      timeout.global_timeout_ = grpc_timeout;\n   215→    } else {\n   216→      timeout.global_timeout_ = route.timeout();\n   217→    }\n   218→  }\n   219→  timeout.per_try_timeout_ = route.retryPolicy().perTryTimeout();\n   220→  timeout.per_try_idle_timeout_ = route.retryPolicy().perTryIdleTimeout();\n   221→\n   222→  uint64_t header_timeout;\n   223→\n   224→  if (respect_expected_rq_timeout) {\n   225→    // Check if there is timeout set by egress Envoy.\n   226→    // If present, use that value as route timeout and don't override\n   227→    // *x-envoy-expected-rq-timeout-ms* header. At this point *x-envoy-upstream-rq-timeout-ms*\n   228→    // header should have been sanitized by egress Envoy.\n   229→    const Http::HeaderEntry* header_expected_timeout_entry =\n   230→        request_headers.EnvoyExpectedRequestTimeoutMs();\n   231→    if (header_expected_timeout_entry) {\n   232→      trySetGlobalTimeout(*header_expected_timeout_entry, timeout);\n   233→    } else {\n   234→      const Http::HeaderEntry* header_timeout_entry =\n   235→          request_headers.EnvoyUpstreamRequestTimeoutMs();\n   236→\n   237→      if (header_timeout_entry) {\n   238→        trySetGlobalTimeout(*header_timeout_entry, timeout);\n   239→        request_headers.removeEnvoyUpstreamRequestTimeoutMs();\n   240→      }\n   241→    }\n   242→  } else {\n   243→    const Http::HeaderEntry* header_timeout_entry = request_headers.EnvoyUpstreamRequestTimeoutMs();\n   244→\n   245→    if (header_timeout_entry) {\n   246→      trySetGlobalTimeout(*header_timeout_entry, timeout);\n   247→      request_headers.removeEnvoyUpstreamRequestTimeoutMs();\n   248→    }\n   249→  }\n   250→\n   251→  // See if there is a per try/retry timeout. If it's >= global we just ignore it.\n   252→  const absl::string_view per_try_timeout_entry =\n   253→      request_headers.getEnvoyUpstreamRequestPerTryTimeoutMsValue();\n   254→  if (!per_try_timeout_entry.empty()) {\n   255→    if (absl::SimpleAtoi(per_try_timeout_entry, &header_timeout)) {\n   256→      timeout.per_try_timeout_ = std::chrono::milliseconds(header_timeout);\n   257→    }\n   258→    request_headers.removeEnvoyUpstreamRequestPerTryTimeoutMs();\n   259→  }\n   260→\n   261→  if (timeout.per_try_timeout_ >= timeout.global_timeout_ && timeout.global_timeout_.count() != 0) {\n   262→    timeout.per_try_timeout_ = std::chrono::milliseconds(0);\n   263→  }\n   264→\n   265→  setTimeoutHeaders(0, timeout, route, request_headers, insert_envoy_expected_request_timeout_ms,\n   266→                    grpc_request, per_try_timeout_hedging_enabled);\n   267→\n   268→  return timeout;\n   269→}\n   270→\n   271→void FilterUtility::setTimeoutHeaders(uint64_t elapsed_time, const TimeoutData& timeout,\n   272→                                      const RouteEntry& route,\n   273→                                      Http::RequestHeaderMap& request_headers,\n   274→                                      bool insert_envoy_expected_request_timeout_ms,\n   275→                                      bool grpc_request, bool per_try_timeout_hedging_enabled) {\n   276→\n   277→  const uint64_t global_timeout = timeout.global_timeout_.count();\n   278→\n   279→  // See if there is any timeout to write in the expected timeout header.\n   280→  uint64_t expected_timeout = timeout.per_try_timeout_.count();\n   281→\n   282→  // Use the global timeout if no per try timeout was specified or if we're\n   283→  // doing hedging when there are per try timeouts. Either of these scenarios\n   284→  // mean that the upstream server can use the full global timeout.\n   285→  if (per_try_timeout_hedging_enabled || expected_timeout == 0) {\n   286→    expected_timeout = global_timeout;\n   287→  }\n   288→\n   289→  // If the expected timeout is 0 set no timeout, as Envoy treats 0 as infinite timeout.\n   290→  if (expected_timeout > 0) {\n   291→\n   292→    if (global_timeout > 0) {\n   293→      if (elapsed_time >= global_timeout) {\n   294→        // We are out of time, but 0 would be an infinite timeout. So instead we send a 1ms timeout\n   295→        // and assume the timers armed by onRequestComplete() will fire very soon.\n   296→        expected_timeout = 1;\n   297→      } else {\n   298→        expected_timeout = std::min(expected_timeout, global_timeout - elapsed_time);\n   299→      }\n   300→    }\n   301→\n   302→    if (insert_envoy_expected_request_timeout_ms) {\n   303→      request_headers.setEnvoyExpectedRequestTimeoutMs(expected_timeout);\n   304→    }\n   305→\n   306→    // If we've configured max_grpc_timeout, override the grpc-timeout header with\n   307→    // the expected timeout. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01A75nZMVJwaWpY4VtxZ9Xet","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→#pragma once\n     2→\n     3→#include <cstdint>\n     4→#include <functional>\n     5→#include <memory>\n     6→#include <string>\n     7→\n     8→#include \"envoy/access_log/access_log.h\"\n     9→#include \"envoy/buffer/buffer.h\"\n    10→#include \"envoy/common/scope_tracker.h\"\n    11→#include \"envoy/event/dispatcher.h\"\n    12→#include \"envoy/grpc/status.h\"\n    13→#include \"envoy/http/codec.h\"\n    14→#include \"envoy/http/filter_factory.h\"\n    15→#include \"envoy/http/header_map.h\"\n    16→#include \"envoy/matcher/matcher.h\"\n    17→#include \"envoy/router/router.h\"\n    18→#include \"envoy/router/scopes.h\"\n    19→#include \"envoy/ssl/connection.h\"\n    20→#include \"envoy/tracing/tracer.h\"\n    21→#include \"envoy/upstream/load_balancer.h\"\n    22→#include \"envoy/upstream/upstream.h\"\n    23→\n    24→#include \"source/common/common/scope_tracked_object_stack.h\"\n    25→\n    26→#include \"absl/types/optional.h\"\n    27→\n    28→namespace Envoy {\n    29→namespace Router {\n    30→class RouteConfigProvider;\n    31→}\n    32→namespace Http {\n    33→\n    34→/**\n    35→ * Return codes for encode/decode headers filter invocations. The connection manager bases further\n    36→ * filter invocations on the return code of the previous filter.\n    37→ */\n    38→enum class FilterHeadersStatus {\n    39→  // Continue filter chain iteration.\n    40→  Continue,\n    41→  // Do not iterate for headers on any of the remaining filters in the chain.\n    42→  //\n    43→  // Returning FilterDataStatus::Continue from decodeData()/encodeData() or calling\n    44→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() MUST be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n    45→  //\n    46→  // Note that if a local reply was sent, no further iteration for headers as well as data and\n    47→  // trailers for the current filter and the filters following will happen. A local reply can be\n    48→  // triggered via sendLocalReply() or encodeHeaders().\n    49→  StopIteration,\n    50→  // Continue headers iteration to remaining filters, but delay ending the stream. This status MUST\n    51→  // NOT be returned when end_stream is already set to false.\n    52→  //\n    53→  // Used when a filter wants to add a body to a headers-only request/response, but this body is not\n    54→  // readily available. Delaying end_stream allows the filter to add the body once it's available\n    55→  // without stopping headers iteration.\n    56→  //\n    57→  // The filter is responsible to continue the stream by providing a body through calling\n    58→  // injectDecodedDataToFilterChain()/injectEncodedDataToFilterChain(), possibly multiple times\n    59→  // if the body needs to be divided into several chunks. The filter may need to handle\n    60→  // watermark events when injecting a body, see:\n    61→  // https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/docs/flow_control.md.\n    62→  //\n    63→  // The last call to inject data MUST have end_stream set to true to conclude the stream.\n    64→  // If the filter cannot provide a body the stream should be reset.\n    65→  //\n    66→  // Adding a body through calling addDecodedData()/addEncodedData() then\n    67→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() is currently NOT supported and causes an assert failure.\n    68→  //\n    69→  // Adding trailers in this scenario is currently NOT supported.\n    70→  //\n    71→  // The filter MUST NOT attempt to continue the stream without providing a body using\n    72→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding().\n    73→  //\n    74→  // TODO(yosrym93): Support adding a body in this case by calling addDecodedData()/addEncodedData()\n    75→  // then continueDecoding()/continueEncoding(). To support this a new FilterManager::IterationState\n    76→  // needs to be added and set when a filter returns this status in\n    77→  // FilterManager::decodeHeaders/FilterManager::encodeHeaders()\n    78→  // Currently, when a filter returns this, the IterationState is Continue. This causes ASSERTs in\n    79→  // FilterManager::commonContinue() to fail when continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() is called;\n    80→  // due to trying to continue iteration when the IterationState is already Continue.\n    81→  // In this case, a different ASSERT will be needed to make sure the filter does not try to\n    82→  // continue without adding a body first.\n    83→  //\n    84→  // TODO(yosrym93): Support adding trailers in this case by implementing new functions to inject\n    85→  // trailers, similar to the inject data functions.\n    86→  ContinueAndDontEndStream,\n    87→  // Do not iterate for headers as well as data and trailers for the current filter and the filters\n    88→  // following, and buffer body data for later dispatching. ContinueDecoding() MUST\n    89→  // be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n    90→  //\n    91→  // Used when a filter wants to stop iteration on data and trailers while waiting for headers'\n    92→  // iteration to resume.\n    93→  //\n    94→  // If buffering the request causes buffered data to exceed the configured buffer limit, a 413 will\n    95→  // be sent to the user. On the response path exceeding buffer limits will result in a 500.\n    96→  //\n    97→  // TODO(soya3129): stop metadata parsing when StopAllIterationAndBuffer is set.\n    98→  StopAllIterationAndBuffer,\n    99→  // Do not iterate for headers as well as data and trailers for the current filter and the filters\n   100→  // following, and buffer body data for later dispatching. continueDecoding() MUST\n   101→  // be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n   102→  //\n   103→  // Used when a filter wants to stop iteration on data and trailers while waiting for headers'\n   104→  // iteration to resume.\n   105→  //\n   106→  // This will cause the flow of incoming data to cease until continueDecoding() function is called.\n   107→  //\n   108→  // TODO(soya3129): stop metadata parsing when StopAllIterationAndWatermark is set.\n   109→  StopAllIterationAndWatermark,\n   110→};\n   111→\n   112→/**\n   113→ * Return codes for encode on informative headers filter invocations.\n   114→ */\n   115→enum class Filter1xxHeadersStatus {\n   116→  // Continue filter chain iteration.\n   117→  Continue,\n   118→  // Do not iterate for informative headers on any of the remaining filters in the chain.\n   119→  //\n   120→  // Returning FilterDataStatus::Continue from encodeData() or calling\n   121→  // continueEncoding() MUST be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n   122→  StopIteration\n   123→};\n   124→\n   125→/**\n   126→ * Return codes for encode/decode data filter invocations. The connection manager bases further\n   127→ * filter invocations on the return code of the previous filter.\n   128→ */\n   129→enum class FilterDataStatus {\n   130→  // Continue filter chain iteration. If headers have not yet been sent to the next filter, they\n   131→  // will be sent first via decodeHeaders()/encodeHeaders(). If data has previously been buffered,\n   132→  // the data in this callback will be added to the buffer before the entirety is sent to the next\n   133→  // filter.\n   134→  Continue,\n   135→  // Do not iterate to any of the remaining filters in the chain, and buffer body data for later\n   136→  // dispatching. Returning FilterDataStatus::Continue from decodeData()/encodeData() or calling\n   137→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() MUST be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n   138→  //\n   139→  // This should be called by filters which must parse a larger block of the incoming data before\n   140→  // continuing processing and so can not push back on streaming data via watermarks.\n   141→  //\n   142→  // If buffering the request causes buffered data to exceed the configured buffer limit, a 413 will\n   143→  // be sent to the user. On the response path exceeding buffer limits will result in a 500.\n   144→  StopIterationAndBuffer,\n   145→  // Do not iterate to any of the remaining filters in the chain, and buffer body data for later\n   146→  // dispatching. Returning FilterDataStatus::Continue from decodeData()/encodeData() or calling\n   147→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() MUST be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n   148→  //\n   149→  // This will cause the flow of incoming data to cease until one of the continue.*() functions is\n   150→  // called.\n   151→  //\n   152→  // This should be returned by filters which can nominally stream data but have a transient back-up\n   153→  // such as the configured delay of the fault filter, or if the router filter is still fetching an\n   154→  // upstream connection.\n   155→  StopIterationAndWatermark,\n   156→  // Do not iterate to any of the remaining filters in the chain, but do not buffer any of the\n   157→  // body data for later dispatching. Returning FilterDataStatus::Continue from\n   158→  // decodeData()/encodeData() or calling continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() MUST be called if\n   159→  // continued filter iteration is desired.\n   160→  //\n   161→  // Note that if a local reply was sent, no further iteration for either data or trailers\n   162→  // for the current filter and the filters following will happen. A local reply can be\n   163→  // triggered via sendLocalReply() or encodeHeaders().\n   164→  StopIterationNoBuffer\n   165→};\n   166→\n   167→/**\n   168→ * Return codes for encode/decode trailers filter invocations. The connection manager bases further\n   169→ * filter invocations on the return code of the previous filter.\n   170→ */\n   171→enum class FilterTrailersStatus {\n   172→  // Continue filter chain iteration.\n   173→  Continue,\n   174→  // Do not iterate to any of the remaining filters in the chain. Calling\n   175→  // continueDecoding()/continueEncoding() MUST be called if continued filter iteration is desired.\n   176→  StopIteration\n   177→};\n   178→\n   179→/**\n   180→ * Return codes for encode metadata filter invocations. Returns a reference to the newly created ActiveStream\n\nThe **ActiveStream** implements:\n- `Http::RequestDecoder` - receives decoded headers/data/trailers from codec\n- `Http::StreamCallbacks` - stream lifecycle callbacks\n- `FilterManagerCallbacks` - interface for filter manager to send responses back\n\n### How does the FilterManager determine the order of decoder filter execution?\n\nThe **FilterManager** class (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.h:600-1090`) maintains a list of decoder filters called **StreamDecoderFilters** (lines 73-81):\n\n```cpp\nstruct StreamDecoderFilters {\n  using Element = ActiveStreamDecoderFilter;\n  using Iterator = std::vector<ActiveStreamDecoderFilterPtr>::iterator;\n  std::vector<ActiveStreamDecoderFilterPtr> entries_;\n};\n```\n\nThe filter order is determined during HTTP filter chain creation. The **HttpConnectionManagerConfig::createFilterChain()** method (`/workspace/source/extensions/filters/network/http_connection_manager/config.cc`) instantiates filters in the order specified in the configuration and adds them to the filter manager via **FilterChainUtility::buildFilterChain()**.\n\nWhen **FilterManager::decodeHeaders()** is called (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:537-636`), it iterates through `decoder_filters_` in forward order (A→B→C):\n```cpp\nfor (; entry != decoder_filters_.end(); entry++) {\n  FilterHeadersStatus status = (*entry)->handle_->decodeHeaders(headers, ...);\n```\n\n### What filter return values can stop or continue iteration?\n\nThe **FilterHeadersStatus** enum (`/workspace/envoy/http/filter.h:38-110`) defines return values:\n\n1. **Continue** - Continue iteration to next filter\n2. **StopIteration** (line 49) - Stop headers iteration, but continue with other filters for body data\n3. **StopAllIterationAndBuffer** (line 98) - Stop all filters, buffer body data for later resumption\n4. **StopAllIterationAndWatermark** (line 109) - Stop all filters, apply flow control watermarks\n5. **ContinueAndDontEndStream** (line 86) - Continue iteration but ignore end_stream flag\n\nThe **FilterDataStatus** enum defines data phase return values:\n1. **Continue** - Continue to next filter\n2. **StopIterationAndBuffer** (line 144) - Stop iteration, buffer remaining data\n3. **StopIterationAndWatermark** (line 155) - Stop iteration, apply watermarks\n4. **StopIterationNoBuffer** (line 164) - Stop iteration without buffering\n\nWhen a filter returns a \"Stop*\" status, the **ActiveStreamFilterBase::commonHandleAfterHeadersCallback()** method (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.h:130-162`) sets the iteration state:\n```cpp\nswitch (status) {\ncase FilterHeadersStatus::StopIteration:\n  iteration_state_ = IterationState::StopSingleIteration;\n  break;\ncase FilterHeadersStatus::StopAllIterationAndBuffer:\n  iteration_state_ = IterationState::StopAllBuffer;\n  break;\n```\n\nIf `stoppedAll()` returns true, iteration stops and returns false to halt the loop.\n\n---\n\n## Q3: Route Resolution and Upstream Selection\n\n### How does the router determine which route configuration to use?\n\nThe **Router filter** (`/workspace/source/common/router/router.h:347`) is a **Http::StreamDecoderFilter** that implements **decodeHeaders()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/router.cc:445-704`). In this method (line 468-477), the router calls:\n\n```cpp\nconst Router::RouteConstSharedPtr route = callbacks_->route();\n```\n\nThis delegates to **FilterManagerCallbacks::route()**, which queries the **ConfigImpl** (the route configuration object). The ConfigImpl uses the **RouteMatcher** to select among multiple virtual hosts and then match routes within the virtual host. The matching occurs in **ConfigImpl::route()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.h:1653-1660`), which takes request headers and stream info and returns the best-matching route entry.\n\nThe route matching logic is implemented in **RouteEntryImplBase::matchRoute()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.cc:913`), which checks:\n- Request path (prefix, exact, regex, or URI template match)\n- Request method (GET, POST, etc.)\n- Request headers (header-matching conditions)\n- Query parameters\n- Runtime conditions\n\n### What is the sequence of operations that resolves a route entry to a specific upstream cluster?\n\nOnce a route is matched, the router accesses its cluster name via **RouteEntryImplBase::clusterName()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.cc:965`), which returns a `const std::string&`.\n\nThen (router.cc line 519):\n```cpp\nconst Upstream::ThreadLocalCluster* cluster =\n    config_->cm_.getThreadLocalCluster(route_entry_->clusterName());\n```\n\nThis looks up the cluster in the thread-local cluster manager cache. If the cluster is not found, the router sends a 503 Service Unavailable response (line 528). The cluster object contains load balancer state, connection pools, and health information for all upstream hosts.\n\nFor dynamic cluster selection (using cluster specifier plugins), the **DynamicRouteEntry** class (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.h:834-983`) is used instead, which determines the cluster name dynamically at request time.\n\n### How does the router select a specific upstream host from the cluster?\n\nIn **Filter::decodeHeaders()** (router.cc line 664), after the cluster is found, the router calls:\n\n```cpp\nauto host_selection_response = cluster->chooseHost(this);\n```\n\nThe **Filter** class extends **Upstream::LoadBalancerContextBase** (router.h line 310) and implements the LoadBalancerContext interface with methods like:\n- `computeHashKey()` (line 363) - For hash-based load balancing\n- `metadataMatchCriteria()` (line 379) - For subset load balancing\n- `downstreamConnection()` (line 404) - Provides downstream connection context\n- `downstreamHeaders()` (line 408) - Provides request headers for matching\n- `shouldSelectAnotherHost()` (line 410) - Retry host selection\n\nThe cluster's load balancer uses these context methods to select an upstream **Upstream::HostConstSharedPtr** from the cluster's healthy hosts. The load balancer may use various algorithms: round-robin, least request, ring hash, maglev, random, etc., depending on the cluster configuration.\n\n### What triggers the creation of the upstream connection?\n\nAfter host selection, the router calls **Filter::continueDecodeHeaders()** (router.cc line 714). This method:\n\n1. **Creates the connection pool** (line 724):\n```cpp\nstd::unique_ptr<GenericConnPool> generic_conn_pool =\n    createConnPool(*cluster, selected_host);\n```\n\n2. This factory is either from the cluster's per-cluster factory or the default HTTP/TCP factory\n\n4. **Creates UpstreamRequest** (router.cc lines 845-847):\n```cpp\nUpstreamRequestPtr upstream_request = std::make_unique<UpstreamRequest>(\n    *this, std::move(generic_conn_pool), can_send_early_data,\n    can_use_http3, allow_multiplexed_upstream_half_close_);\n```\n\n5. **Initiates the connection** (line 849):\n```cpp\nupstream_requests_.front()->acceptHeadersFromRouter(end_stream);\n```\n\nThe UpstreamRequest's `acceptHeadersFromRouter()` method (`/workspace/source/common/router/upstream_request.cc:380-434`) calls:\n```cpp\nconn_pool_->newStream(this)\n```\n\nThis call to `GenericConnPool::newStream()` (`/workspace/envoy/router/router.h:1438`) triggers the connection pool to either reuse an existing upstream connection or create a new one. The pool returns a callback interface, and when the connection is ready (or fails), it invokes **GenericConnectionPoolCallbacks::onPoolReady()** or **onPoolFailure()**.\n\n---\n\n## Q4: Upstream Connection and Data Flow\n\n### How does the connection pool provide or create an upstream connection?\n\nThe **ConnPoolImplBase::tryCreateNewConnection()** method (`/workspace/source/common/conn_pool/conn_pool_base.cc:164-201`) handles upstream connection creation. When `newStream()` is called on the pool:\n\n1. The pool checks if it can reuse an existing connection or must create a new one\n2. If a new connection is needed, `tryCreateNewConnection()` is called\n3. This calls the pure virtual `instantiateActiveClient()` method (line 182), which is overridden by HTTP and TCP pool implementations\n4. For HTTP pools, **HttpConnPoolImplBase** (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_pool_base.h:49-103`) creates an **ActiveClient** (lines 107-124)\n5. The ActiveClient's constructor initiates a network connection to the upstream host\n\nThe **ConnectionImpl::onEvent()** callback in the upstream socket notifies the pool when the connection is established. Once established, the pool transitions the ActiveClient to the **Ready** state and invokes the waiting stream's **onPoolReady()** callback.\n\n### What component actually writes the HTTP request to the upstream socket?\n\nThe **UpstreamRequest** class implements **RequestDecoder** for receiving upstream responses, but for sending requests, it receives an **Http::RequestEncoder** from the connection pool's `onPoolReady()` callback.\n\nIn **HttpConnPoolImplBase::onPoolReady()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_pool_base.cc:84-94`):\n```cpp\nvoid HttpConnPoolImplBase::onPoolReady(...) {\n  ActiveClient* http_client = static_cast<ActiveClient*>(&client);\n  Http::RequestEncoder& new_encoder = http_client->newStreamEncoder(response_decoder);\n  callbacks.onPoolReady(new_encoder, ...);\n}\n```\n\nThe `newStreamEncoder()` creates an encoder for the upstream codec (HTTP/1 or HTTP/2). The UpstreamRequest then:\n\n1. Calls **UpstreamRequest::acceptHeadersFromRouter()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/upstream_request.cc:380-434`), which forwards the request headers through the **UpstreamFilterManager**\n2. The filter manager's `decodeHeaders()` call encodes the headers via the RequestEncoder\n3. The RequestEncoder writes to the underlying HTTP codec (HTTP1::ServerConnection or HTTP2::ServerConnection)\n4. The codec writes the HTTP-formatted request to the upstream **Network::Connection**'s write buffer\n5. The network connection's write event loop transmits the data via the TCP socket\n\n### How does the response flow back from upstream through the filter chain?\n\nWhen the upstream server sends a response:\n\n1. The TCP socket receives data and calls the HCM's **onData()** callback\n2. The HCM's codec (on the upstream connection) parses the response headers/body\n3. The codec invokes its `ServerConnectionCallbacks::newStream()` method, which creates an UpstreamRequest acting as a ResponseDecoder\n4. The UpstreamRequest's **decodeHeaders()** method (`/workspace/source/common/router/upstream_request.cc:267-307`) receives the response headers\n5. This method calls `parent_.onUpstreamHeaders()` to notify the Router filter\n6. The Router filter invokes **ActiveStream::encodeHeaders()** to start the downstream encoder filter chain\n7. The encoder filter chain processes the response headers in **reverse order** (C→B→A)\n8. Each encoder filter's `encodeHeaders()` method is called, and the final result is written to the downstream ResponseEncoder\n9. The downstream codec (HTTP/1 or HTTP/2) formats the headers and writes them to the downstream connection\n\nSimilarly for body data and trailers, UpstreamRequest's **decodeData()** and **decodeTrailers()** methods invoke the encoder filter chain via **ActiveStream::encodeData()** and **encodeTrailers()**.\n\n### At what points does the encoder filter chain get invoked vs. the decoder filter chain?\n\n**Decoder Filter Chain** (Request Path - Forward Order A→B→C):\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::decodeHeaders()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:537-636`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::decodeData()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:638-762`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::decodeTrailers()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:764-844`)\n- Entry point: Codec signals new request → **ActiveStream::decodeHeaders()** → **FilterManager::decodeHeaders()**\n\n**Encoder Filter Chain** (Response Path - **REVERSE Order** C→B→A):\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::encodeHeaders()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:1225-1314`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::encodeData()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:1316-1399`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::encodeTrailers()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:1401-1480`)\n- Entry point: Upstream response received → **UpstreamRequest::decodeHeaders()** → **Router::onUpstreamHeaders()** → **ActiveStream::encodeHeaders()** → **FilterManager::encodeHeaders()**\n\nThe **StreamEncoderFilters** structure (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.h:92-100`) uses a **reverse_iterator**, and `begin()` calls `rbegin()`, iterating from last filter to first:\n```cpp\nIterator begin() { return entries_.rbegin(); }  // Reverse iteration!\n```\n\nThe **commonEncodePrefix()** method (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:900-932`) explicitly returns `encoder_filters_.begin()` which is the reverse begin, starting iteration from the end of the configured filter list.\n\n---\n\n## Evidence\n\n### Critical File References\n\n#### Network Listener and Filter Chain Selection\n- `/workspace/envoy/network/listener.h:310-335` - TcpListenerCallbacks interface\n- The **ActiveTcpListener** class (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/active_tcp_listener.h:25-99`) implements this interface and is the primary component that accepts incoming TCP connections through its `onAccept(Network::ConnectionSocketPtr&& socket)` method (line 54).\n\nWhen a socket accepts a connection, the event loop calls `TcpListener::onAccept()`, which delegates to `ActiveTcpListener::onAccept()` (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/active_tcp_listener.cc:80-91`). This method:\n1. Checks listener connection limits\n2. Calls `onAcceptWorker(socket, config_->handOffRestoredDestinationConnections(), false)` (line 91)\n\n### How does Envoy select which network filter chain to apply based on connection properties?\n\nThe network filter chain selection occurs after listener filters complete, in the **ActiveStreamListenerBase::newConnection()** method (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/active_stream_listener_base.cc:38-76`). This method calls:\n\n```cpp\nconfig_->filterChainManager().findFilterChain(*socket, *stream_info)\n```\n\nThe **FilterChainManager::findFilterChain()** method (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/filter_chain_manager_impl.h:148-149`) uses connection properties to select the appropriate filter chain:\n- Destination port/IP address\n- Server name (SNI for TLS connections)\n- Transport protocol\n- Application protocols\n- Source IPs and ports\n- Connection source type (from proxy protocol, etc.)\n\n### At what point does the connection get handed to the HTTP Connection Manager (HCM)?\n\nThe handoff occurs during network filter chain creation in **ListenerImpl::createNetworkFilterChain()** (`/workspace/source/common/listener_manager/listener_impl.cc:942-952`). This method calls:\n\n```cpp\nConfiguration::FilterChainUtility::buildFilterChain(connection, filter_factories)\n```\n\nThe filter factories list contains the HTTP Connection Manager factory. The **HttpConnectionManagerFilterConfigFactory::createFilterFactoryFromProtoTyped()** (`/workspace/source/extensions/filters/network/http_connection_manager/config.cc`) creates a filter factory callback that instantiates the HTTP Connection Manager network filter. Once instantiated, the HCM becomes a **Network::ReadFilter** on the connection and receives all subsequent data.\n\n### What triggers the initial onData() call when bytes arrive?\n\nOnce the connection is fully established with all network filters instantiated, the underlying **Network::Connection** object's read event loop triggers when bytes arrive from the TCP socket. The network event dispatcher calls the registered **Network::ReadFilter::onData(Buffer::Instance& data, bool end_stream)** method on each filter in the filter chain.\n\nFor the HTTP Connection Manager, this triggers **ConnectionManagerImpl::onData()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_manager_impl.cc:486-542`). This is the entry point where raw TCP bytes are handed to the HTTP parsing layer.\n\n---\n\n## Q2: HTTP Parsing and Filter Chain Iteration\n\n### How does the codec parse the byte stream into HTTP headers/body?\n\nThe HTTP codec is created lazily in **ConnectionManagerImpl::onData()** (line 496 of `conn_manager_impl.cc`) on first data arrival via:\n```cpp\ncodec_ = config_->createCodec(connection_, data, *this, overload_manager_)\n```\n\nThe codec factory creates either:\n- **Http1::ServerConnectionImpl** (`/workspace/source/common/http/http1/codec_impl.h:465-583`) for HTTP/1.1\n- **Http2::ServerConnectionImpl** (`/workspace/source/common/http/http2/codec_impl.h:815-879`) for HTTP/2\n\nOnce created, `codec_->dispatch(data)` is called repeatedly in `onData()` (line 503). The codec uses internal state machines to parse bytes:\n\n**For HTTP/1.1** (`/workspace/source/common/http/http1/codec_impl.cc`):\n- Parser callbacks invoke `onMessageBeginBase()` (line 1266) when HTTP message starts\n- `onHeadersCompleteBase()` (line 1251) after headers parsed\n- `onBody()` (line 1290) for body data\n- `onMessageCompleteBase()` (line 1325) when message complete\n\n**For HTTP/2** (`/workspace/source/common/http/http2/codec_impl.h`):\n- Http2Visitor implements frame callbacks: `OnBeginHeadersForStream()`, `OnHeaderForStream()`, `OnEndHeadersForStream()`, `OnDataForStream()`, `OnEndStream()` (lines 191-258)\n\n### When is the ActiveStream created and how does it relate to filter iteration?\n\nWhen the codec detects a new HTTP message, it calls the **ServerConnectionCallbacks::newStream()** method. This is implemented by **ConnectionManagerImpl::newStream()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_manager_impl.cc:387-442`).\n\nThe `newStream()` method:\n1. Creates an **ActiveStream** struct (line 407), which is a private struct inside ConnectionManagerImpl (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_manager_impl.h:141-532`)\n2. Initializes the stream's filter manager (line 430)\n3. Sets up response encoder callbacks (lines 431-434)\n4. Adds the stream to the active streams list (line 440)\n5. Returns a reference to the newly created ActiveStream\n\nThe **ActiveStream** implements:\n- `Http::RequestDecoder` - receives decoded headers/data/trailers from codec\n- `Http::StreamCallbacks` - stream lifecycle callbacks\n- `FilterManagerCallbacks` - interface for filter manager to send responses back\n\n### How does the FilterManager determine the order of decoder filter execution?\n\nThe **FilterManager** class (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.h:600-1090`) maintains a list of decoder filters called **StreamDecoderFilters** (lines 73-81):\n\n```cpp\nstruct StreamDecoderFilters {\n  using Element = ActiveStreamDecoderFilter;\n  using Iterator = std::vector<ActiveStreamDecoderFilterPtr>::iterator;\n  std::vector<ActiveStreamDecoderFilterPtr> entries_;\n};\n```\n\nThe filter order is determined during HTTP filter chain creation. The **HttpConnectionManagerConfig::createFilterChain()** method (`/workspace/source/extensions/filters/network/http_connection_manager/config.cc`) instantiates filters in the order specified in the configuration and adds them to the filter manager via **FilterChainUtility::buildFilterChain()**.\n\nWhen **FilterManager::decodeHeaders()** is called (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:537-636`), it iterates through `decoder_filters_` in forward order (A→B→C):\n```cpp\nfor (; entry != decoder_filters_.end(); entry++) {\n  FilterHeadersStatus status = (*entry)->handle_->decodeHeaders(headers, ...);\n```\n\n### What filter return values can stop or continue iteration?\n\nThe **FilterHeadersStatus** enum (`/workspace/envoy/http/filter.h:38-110`) defines return values:\n\n1. **Continue** - Continue iteration to next filter\n2. **StopIteration** (line 49) - Stop headers iteration, but continue with other filters for body data\n3. **StopAllIterationAndBuffer** (line 98) - Stop all filters, buffer body data for later resumption\n4. **StopAllIterationAndWatermark** (line 109) - Stop all filters, apply flow control watermarks\n5. **ContinueAndDontEndStream** (line 86) - Continue iteration but ignore end_stream flag\n\nThe **FilterDataStatus** enum defines data phase return values:\n1. **Continue** - Continue to next filter\n2. **StopIterationAndBuffer** (line 144) - Stop iteration, buffer remaining data\n3. **StopIterationAndWatermark** (line 155) - Stop iteration, apply watermarks\n4. **StopIterationNoBuffer** (line 164) - Stop iteration without buffering\n\nWhen a filter returns a \"Stop*\" status, the **ActiveStreamFilterBase::commonHandleAfterHeadersCallback()** method (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.h:130-162`) sets the iteration state:\n```cpp\nswitch (status) {\ncase FilterHeadersStatus::StopIteration:\n  iteration_state_ = IterationState::StopSingleIteration;\n  break;\ncase FilterHeadersStatus::StopAllIterationAndBuffer:\n  iteration_state_ = IterationState::StopAllBuffer;\n  break;\n```\n\nIf `stoppedAll()` returns true, iteration stops and returns false to halt the loop.\n\n---\n\n## Q3: Route Resolution and Upstream Selection\n\n### How does the router determine which route configuration to use?\n\nThe **Router filter** (`/workspace/source/common/router/router.h:347`) is a **Http::StreamDecoderFilter** that implements **decodeHeaders()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/router.cc:445-704`). In this method (line 468-477), the router calls:\n\n```cpp\nconst Router::RouteConstSharedPtr route = callbacks_->route();\n```\n\nThis delegates to **FilterManagerCallbacks::route()**, which queries the **ConfigImpl** (the route configuration object). The ConfigImpl uses the **RouteMatcher** to select among multiple virtual hosts and then match routes within the virtual host. The matching occurs in **ConfigImpl::route()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.h:1653-1660`), which takes request headers and stream info and returns the best-matching route entry.\n\nThe route matching logic is implemented in **RouteEntryImplBase::matchRoute()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.cc:913`), which checks:\n- Request path (prefix, exact, regex, or URI template match)\n- Request method (GET, POST, etc.)\n- Request headers (header-matching conditions)\n- Query parameters\n- Runtime conditions\n\n### What is the sequence of operations that resolves a route entry to a specific upstream cluster?\n\nOnce a route is matched, the router accesses its cluster name via **RouteEntryImplBase::clusterName()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.cc:965`), which returns a `const std::string&`.\n\nThen (router.cc line 519):\n```cpp\nconst Upstream::ThreadLocalCluster* cluster =\n    config_->cm_.getThreadLocalCluster(route_entry_->clusterName());\n```\n\nThis looks up the cluster in the thread-local cluster manager cache. If the cluster is not found, the router sends a 503 Service Unavailable response (line 528). The cluster object contains load balancer state, connection pools, and health information for all upstream hosts.\n\nFor dynamic cluster selection (using cluster specifier plugins), the **DynamicRouteEntry** class (`/workspace/source/common/router/config_impl.h:834-983`) is used instead, which determines the cluster name dynamically at request time.\n\n### How does the router select a specific upstream host from the cluster?\n\nIn **Filter::decodeHeaders()** (router.cc line 664), after the cluster is found, the router calls:\n\n```cpp\nauto host_selection_response = cluster->chooseHost(this);\n```\n\nThe **Filter** class extends **Upstream::LoadBalancerContextBase** (router.h line 310) and implements the LoadBalancerContext interface with methods like:\n- `computeHashKey()` (line 363) - For hash-based load balancing\n- `metadataMatchCriteria()` (line 379) - For subset load balancing\n- `downstreamConnection()` (line 404) - Provides downstream connection context\n- `downstreamHeaders()` (line 408) - Provides request headers for matching\n- `shouldSelectAnotherHost()` (line 410) - Retry host selection\n\nThe cluster's load balancer uses these context methods to select an upstream **Upstream::HostConstSharedPtr** from the cluster's healthy hosts. The load balancer may use various algorithms: round-robin, least request, ring hash, maglev, random, etc., depending on the cluster configuration.\n\n### What triggers the creation of the upstream connection?\n\nAfter host selection, the router calls **Filter::continueDecodeHeaders()** (router.cc line 714). This method:\n\n1. **Creates the connection pool** (line 724):\n```cpp\nstd::unique_ptr<GenericConnPool> generic_conn_pool =\n    createConnPool(*cluster, selected_host);\n```\n\n2. The **GenericConnPool** is created by the **GenericConnPoolFactory** (`/workspace/envoy/router/router.h:1595-1613`), which calls:\n```cpp\nfactory->createGenericConnPool(host, cluster, upstream_protocol, ...)\n```\n\n3. This factory is either from the cluster's per-cluster factory or the default HTTP/TCP factory\n\n4. **Creates UpstreamRequest** (router.cc lines 845-847):\n```cpp\nUpstreamRequestPtr upstream_request = std::make_unique<UpstreamRequest>(\n    *this, std::move(generic_conn_pool), can_send_early_data,\n    can_use_http3, allow_multiplexed_upstream_half_close_);\n```\n\n5. **Initiates the connection** (line 849):\n```cpp\nupstream_requests_.front()->acceptHeadersFromRouter(end_stream);\n```\n\nThe UpstreamRequest's `acceptHeadersFromRouter()` method (`/workspace/source/common/router/upstream_request.cc:380-434`) calls:\n```cpp\nconn_pool_->newStream(this)\n```\n\nThis call to `GenericConnPool::newStream()` (`/workspace/envoy/router/router.h:1438`) triggers the connection pool to either reuse an existing upstream connection or create a new one. The pool returns a callback interface, and when the connection is ready (or fails), it invokes **GenericConnectionPoolCallbacks::onPoolReady()** or **onPoolFailure()**.\n\n---\n\n## Q4: Upstream Connection and Data Flow\n\n### How does the connection pool provide or create an upstream connection?\n\nThe **ConnPoolImplBase::tryCreateNewConnection()** method (`/workspace/source/common/conn_pool/conn_pool_base.cc:164-201`) handles upstream connection creation. When `newStream()` is called on the pool:\n\n1. The pool checks if it can reuse an existing connection or must create a new one\n2. If a new connection is needed, `tryCreateNewConnection()` is called\n3. This calls the pure virtual `instantiateActiveClient()` method (line 182), which is overridden by HTTP and TCP pool implementations\n4. For HTTP pools, **HttpConnPoolImplBase** (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_pool_base.h:49-103`) creates an **ActiveClient** (lines 107-124)\n5. The ActiveClient's constructor initiates a network connection to the upstream host\n\nThe **ConnectionImpl::onEvent()** callback in the upstream socket notifies the pool when the connection is established. Once established, the pool transitions the ActiveClient to the **Ready** state and invokes the waiting stream's **onPoolReady()** callback.\n\n### What component actually writes the HTTP request to the upstream socket?\n\nThe **UpstreamRequest** class implements **RequestDecoder** for receiving upstream responses, but for sending requests, it receives an **Http::RequestEncoder** from the connection pool's `onPoolReady()` callback.\n\nIn **HttpConnPoolImplBase::onPoolReady()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/conn_pool_base.cc:84-94`):\n```cpp\nvoid HttpConnPoolImplBase::onPoolReady(...) {\n  ActiveClient* http_client = static_cast<ActiveClient*>(&client);\n  Http::RequestEncoder& new_encoder = http_client->newStreamEncoder(response_decoder);\n  callbacks.onPoolReady(new_encoder, ...);\n}\n```\n\nThe `newStreamEncoder()` creates an encoder for the upstream codec (HTTP/1 or HTTP/2). The UpstreamRequest then:\n\n1. Calls **UpstreamRequest::acceptHeadersFromRouter()** (`/workspace/source/common/router/upstream_request.cc:380-434`), which forwards the request headers through the **UpstreamFilterManager**\n2. The filter manager's `decodeHeaders()` call encodes the headers via the RequestEncoder\n3. The RequestEncoder writes to the underlying HTTP codec (HTTP1::ServerConnection or HTTP2::ServerConnection)\n4. The codec writes the HTTP-formatted request to the upstream **Network::Connection**'s write buffer\n5. The network connection's write event loop transmits the data via the TCP socket\n\n### How does the response flow back from upstream through the filter chain?\n\nWhen the upstream server sends a response:\n\n1. The TCP socket receives data and calls the HCM's **onData()** callback\n2. The HCM's codec (on the upstream connection) parses the response headers/body\n3. The codec invokes its `ServerConnectionCallbacks::newStream()` method, which creates an UpstreamRequest acting as a ResponseDecoder\n4. The UpstreamRequest's **decodeHeaders()** method (`/workspace/source/common/router/upstream_request.cc:267-307`) receives the response headers\n5. This method calls `parent_.onUpstreamHeaders()` to notify the Router filter\n6. The Router filter invokes **ActiveStream::encodeHeaders()** to start the downstream encoder filter chain\n7. The encoder filter chain processes the response headers in **reverse order** (C→B→A)\n8. Each encoder filter's `encodeHeaders()` method is called, and the final result is written to the downstream ResponseEncoder\n9. The downstream codec (HTTP/1 or HTTP/2) formats the headers and writes them to the downstream connection\n\nSimilarly for body data and trailers, UpstreamRequest's **decodeData()** and **decodeTrailers()** methods invoke the encoder filter chain via **ActiveStream::encodeData()** and **encodeTrailers()**.\n\n### At what points does the encoder filter chain get invoked vs. the decoder filter chain?\n\n**Decoder Filter Chain** (Request Path - Forward Order A→B→C):\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::decodeHeaders()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:537-636`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::decodeData()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:638-762`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::decodeTrailers()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:764-844`)\n- Entry point: Codec signals new request → **ActiveStream::decodeHeaders()** → **FilterManager::decodeHeaders()**\n\n**Encoder Filter Chain** (Response Path - **REVERSE Order** C→B→A):\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::encodeHeaders()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:1225-1314`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::encodeData()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:1316-1399`)\n- Invoked in **FilterManager::encodeTrailers()** (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:1401-1480`)\n- Entry point: Upstream response received → **UpstreamRequest::decodeHeaders()** → **Router::onUpstreamHeaders()** → **ActiveStream::encodeHeaders()** → **FilterManager::encodeHeaders()**\n\nThe **StreamEncoderFilters** structure (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.h:92-100`) uses a **reverse_iterator**, and `begin()` calls `rbegin()`, iterating from last filter to first:\n```cpp\nIterator begin() { return entries_.rbegin(); }  // Reverse iteration!\n```\n\nThe **commonEncodePrefix()** method (`/workspace/source/common/http/filter_manager.cc:900-932`) explicitly returns `encoder_filters_.begin()` which is the reverse begin, starting iteration from the end of the configured filter list.\n\n---\n\n## Evidence\n\n### Critical File References\n\n#### Network Listener and Filter Chain Selection\n- `/workspace/envoy/network/listener.h:310-335` - TcpListenerCallbacks interface\n-
